coredata.dc:title: Crosslinked fish scale gelatin/alginate dialdehyde functional films incorporated with carbon dots derived from pomelo peel waste for active food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yongshi
Yang, Junxian
Sun, Linping
Liu, Bingzhen
Li, Hui
Peng, Lincai
coredata.dc:description: A multifunctional and environmentally friendly composite film was developed by incorporating pomelo peel-derived carbon dots (PCDs) into a fish scale gelatin (FSG)/alginate dialdehyde (ADA) biopolymer matrix. ADA was used to reinforce the physicomechanical properties of the FSG film via Schiff base crosslinking. PCDs with strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The effect of various PCDs content on the surface morphological, physicochemical, and functional characteristics of the composite films was investigated. The results showed that the introduction of PCDs into the FSG/ADA matrix effectively reinforced the mechanical performance, enhanced the water vapor and water resistance, increased UV-light blocking, conferred fluorescence properties, and improved the thermal properties of the composite films. Under 3 wt% PCDs content, the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % composite film not only presented significant antioxidant capacity with a radical scavenging rate of 91.71 % for DPPH and approximately 100 % for ABTS, but also exhibited excellent antimicrobial ability against bacteria and fungi. Results of a preservation experiment showed that the prepared FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film preserved the physiological qualities of strawberries post-harvest and extended their shelf-life to 7 days at room temperature. Overall, the fabricated FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films are promising for use in eco-friendly active food packaging.

head.ce:title: Crosslinked fish scale gelatin/alginate dialdehyde functional films incorporated with carbon dots derived from pomelo peel waste for active food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional and environmentally friendly composite film was developed by incorporating pomelo peel-derived carbon dots (PCDs) into a fish scale gelatin (FSG)/alginate dialdehyde (ADA) biopolymer matrix. ADA was used to reinforce the physicomechanical properties of the FSG film via Schiff base crosslinking. PCDs with strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The effect of various PCDs content on the surface morphological, physicochemical, and functional characteristics of the composite films was investigated. The results showed that the introduction of PCDs into the FSG/ADA matrix effectively reinforced the mechanical performance, enhanced the water vapor and water resistance, increased UV-light blocking, conferred fluorescence properties, and improved the thermal properties of the composite films. Under 3 wt% PCDs content, the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % composite film not only presented significant antioxidant capacity with a radical scavenging rate of 91.71 % for DPPH and approximately 100 % for ABTS, but also exhibited excellent antimicrobial ability against bacteria and fungi. Results of a preservation experiment showed that the prepared FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film preserved the physiological qualities of strawberries post-harvest and extended their shelf-life to 7 days at room temperature. Overall, the fabricated FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films are promising for use in eco-friendly active food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The overutilization of fossil-based plastic packaging has severely threatened the environmental and ecological sustainability, thus prompting researchers to develop renewable and sustainable packaging as a substitute [1–4]. Recently, the development of biopolymer-based packaging materials has received considerable interest owing to their accessibility, biodegradability, and renewability [5]. Among natural resources, residual biomass from industrial and agricultural waste has been identified as an excellent source of biopolymers and organic carbon [6]. It not only alleviates environmental and resource pressures, but also benefits high-value utilization of the residual biomass.

In recent years, mammalian gelatin, which is a protein-based biopolymer derived primarily from the waste (such as pig skin, cattle bones and cattle hide) of the meat processing industry, has been widely utilized in food packaging because of its good film-forming properties, colorlessness, tastelessness, translucency, UV resistance, and biodegradability [7,8]. However, the utilization of mammalian gelatin is restricted due to religious concerns and the potential threat of outbreaks related to swine plague and bovine spongiform encephalopathy [9]. Therefore, the use of other sources of gelatin become increasing and meaningful. During the fish processing, many by-products, including fish scale, bone, and skin, are generated and are typically disposed of simply by throwing or reused to obtain low-value products, including livestock feed or fertilizer. Among the by-products, the fish scales contain 30 %–40 % collagen in dry weight, and further processing to yield gelatin not only reduces bioresource wastages, but also mitigates environmental pollution [10]. Nevertheless, individual fish scale gelatin (FSG) films are inferior in terms of mechanical properties, barrier properties, and waterproofing, thus they cannot satisfy the requirements of wrapping and barrier capabilities for packaging films [11]. An effective strategy is to improve the polymer network by introducing intra- and intermolecular chemical linkages via aldehyde-based crosslinking process [12,13]. In contrast to the toxic chemical aldehyde reagents, polysaccharide aldehydes have evolved as unique bio-crosslinkers owing to their regulated reactivity, biological compatibility, and negligible toxic effects [14]. Alginate dialdehyde (ADA) is an aldehyde polysaccharide with multiple functional aldehyde groups that can react with the amino groups to yield a Schiff base bond [15,16]. Some studies showed that incorporating of ADA into protein-based films can increase their mechanical strength, water resistance, and water vapor barrier properties [17,18].

Furthermore, the incorporation of functional nanomaterials such as nanoemulsions [19], zinc oxide [20], polydopamine nanoparticles [21], and carbon dots (CDs) [22] into biopolymer-based films is an effective approach for enhancing the performances of packaging materials. Among them, as a new family of carbon materials, CDs that possess a carbonaceous core and surface functional groups have received numerous interest as nanofillers for the development of active packaging because of their unique advantages, including excellent biocompatibility, UV protection, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, low toxicity, and simple synthesis methods [23,24]. Although various carbon sources have been utilized to manufacture CDs, CDs derived from natural bioresources have received significant interest due to their abundance, sustainability, and low cost. As a major by-product of pomelo processing, pomelo peel constitutes 30 %–50 % of the total weight of the pomelo, which is usually discarded as waste or used as animal feed [25]. These treatments not only deliver minimal added value, but also adversely affect the environment. Pomelo peel waste contains a large amount of cellulose, pectin, essential oils, and various phytochemicals with polyphenol as their main component [26]. Thus, pomelo peel waste is one of the most accessible and collectible agricultural wastes that can be utilized as a carbon precursor. It has been reported that CDs derived from pomelo peels have been utilized as fluorescent materials for the selective detection of tetracycline [27], whereas CDs derived from pomelo peel waste as nanofillers for active packaging are yet to be reported. The synthesis of CDs for the development of functional nanofillers for food packaging offers a promising high-value utilization of pomelo peel waste.

In this study, we prepared an active packaging film with multifunctional properties via the solution- casting method using FSG and ADA as the matrix, pomelo peel-derived CDs (PCDs) as functional nanofillers. The physicomechanical, optical, thermal, and functional properties of the developed FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films were systematically evaluated. The overall migration of the FSG/ADA/PCDs film was studied. Packaging applications for strawberry preservation were conducted, the appearance, nutrimental and microbial characteristics of the strawberries were measured during 7 days storage at room temperature.

Tilapia fish scales were provided by Longzhirun Aquatic Products Co., Ltd. (Zhanjiang, China) Pomelo peel was collected from a wholesale fruit and vegetable market in Kunming, China. Sodium alginate, sodium periodate (99 %), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS), terephthalic acid (TPA) and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemical reagents were analytically pure and were used without further purification.

First, the fish scales were cleaned with deionized water to remove impurities, followed by drying in a hot oven at 60 °C. After treatment with 3.5 % (v/v) HCl at a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:10 (g/mL) for 1.5 h, the fish scales were then washed with deionized water until a neutral pH was achieved. Subsequently, the treated fish scales were extracted by boiling in water for 1 h at a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:20 (g/mL). The filtrate was collected and lyophilized to obtain FSG powder. The yield of FSG was measured to be 78.2 % based on the dry weight of the fish scales.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of alginate dialdehyde (ADA)
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium alginate (10 g) was dissolved in deionized water (200 mL) and sodium periodate (5.404 g) was dissolved in deionized water (50 mL). These two solutions were mixed and stirred continuously for 6 h in the dark at room temperature. Subsequently, ethylene glycol (3.5 mL) was added, and stirring was continued for 30 min to halt the reaction. The resultant mixture was dialyzed with deionized water for 7 d to remove residual periodate, followed by lyophilization to obtain ADA powder. The ADA yield was determined to be 56.3 % based on dry weight of alginate. The aldehyde content of the ADA was measured to be 3.62 mmol/g based on the titration method described by Ding et al. [28].

The PCDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2 g of pomelo peel powder was dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water and then transferred to a stainless-steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon). Subsequently, it was heated to 200 °C, and the temperature was maintained as such for 6 h using a silicone oil bath. After cooling to room temperature, the resulted mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was obtained and filtered immediately using a glass sand core funnel with a 0.22 μm filtration membrane. Finally, the resulting filtrate was lyophilized to obtain brown PCD powder. The PCD powder was stored in a freezer until further characterization and use.

The morphology of PCDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM, Talos F200S, FEI, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the PCD solution was scanned in the range of 200–800 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PCDs was examined using an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 2θ from 5° to 90°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet Is5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) was used to examine the chemical groups of the PCDs within the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber region. The fluorescence properties of the PCDs solutions were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Germany). The antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of the PCDs were investigated, and the methods used are detailed in the Experimental section of Supplementary Materials in details.

FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films were fabricated using the conventional solvent-casting method. FSG powder was dispersed in deionized water to prepare a 2 wt% FSG solution, which was then heated at 50 °C while being constantly stirred. ADA powder was dissolved in deionized water to achieve a 2 wt% ADA solution. Subsequently, the ADA and FSG solutions were blended at a mass ratio of 1:1 (the mass ratio was obtained by optimizing the mechanical properties (the results are shown in Supplementary Table S1), 30 wt% glycerol (based on FSG/ADA) was added as a plasticize, and the mixture was constantly stirred for 60 min to achieve an FSG/ADA film-forming solution. Different PCD contents (0, 1, 3, 5, and 7 wt% based on the FSG/ADA) were dispersed uniformly in the FSG/ADA film-forming solution under vigorous agitation for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was sonicated for 15 min to eliminate bubbles therein. Next, 100 g of the mixture was cast on a custom-developed square Teflon plate (edge length of 20 cm) and dried in a fume cupboard at room temperature. All the film samples were peeled off gently from plates and stored in a sealed desiccator for 48 h under 50 % relative humidity (RH) at ∼25 °C to balance the moisture content of the films prior to testing. The prepared films were termed as FSG/ADA, FSG/ADA/PCDs-1 %, FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 %, FSG/ADA/PCDs5-%, and FSG/ADA/PCDs-7 % based on their PCDs content. The preparation process of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films were simply illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), XRD and FTIR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: SEM (MIRA LMS, Tescan, Czech) was used to analyze the surface morphologies of the composite films. Gold was first sprayed onto each sample and then observed at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The crystalline pattern of films was observed using an Ultima IV diffractometer with a monochromatized Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) source in the 2θ range from 5° to 90°at 40 kV. A Nicolet Is5 FTIR spectrometer with ATR attachment was used to analyze the chemical groups of films in the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range.

The film thicknesses were measured using a portable digital micrometer. Ten random positions were selected on each film. Mechanical strength tests were conducted using a QLW-5E universal tensile machine (Qunlong Instruments Co., Ltd., Xiamen, China). Three mechanical parameters of the samples, i.e., tensile strength (TS), elastic modulus (EM), and elongation at break (EB), were measured in accordance with the ASTM Standard Method D 882. 10 (ASTM, 2001). During the test, a rectangular strip (1.5 cm × 8 cm) of the film sample was mounted in the grips of the machine; the initial grips length was 50 mm, and the stretching speed was set at 50 mm/min until breaking occurred. A minimum of five parallel measurements were performed for each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
The WVP of film samples was measured using a gravimetric method in accordance with ASTM E96-92 [29]. Anhydrous pre-dried silica gels were placed in a weighing bottle (2.5 cm diameter), and the tested film sample was tightly covered on the weighing bottle mouth using a rubber ring. Subsequently, the weighing bottles were transferred to a 75 % RH desiccator pre-equilibrated with supersaturated NaCl solution (25 °C). The bottles were weighed at preset times until the weight was almost constant. The WVP of the film samples were measured using Eq. (1):
(1)
WVP
g ⋅ m /
m 2
⋅ Pa ⋅ s
=
Δm × d
Δt × A × ΔP
where ∆P (Pa), d (m), A (m2), and ∆m/∆t (g/s) represent the difference in water vapor pressure at 25 °C between two sides of the film, the film thickness, the effective permeated area, and the weight increase in units of time, respectively.
The water resistance of different films was evaluated based on the water solubility (WS) and water contact angle (WCA). The methods are described in detail in our previous report [30].

The light transmittance of films was examined in the 200–800 nm region using a T90 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). An empty quartz cell was used as reference. A 3NH portable colorimeter (NR 110, 3nh Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China) was used to measure the apparent color of the films. A standard whiteboard (L* = 99.99, a* = 0.18, and b* = −0.86) was used as reference. Five replicates were analyzed for each film sample. The total color difference (∆E) was measured using Eq. (2):
(2)

2

2
where L, a and b are the color parameters of film samples.

A spectrofluorometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Germany) was used to measure the fluorescence of FSG/ADA/PCDs film solution. Photographs of the FSG/ADA/PCDs films with different PCD contents were captured under UV light (365 nm).

The thermal characteristics of films were determined using a thermo-gravimetric analyzer (TG 209 F1; Netzsch, Germany). Under a nitrogen environment, approximately 10 mg of the film samples was placed into an alumina crucible and heated from room temperature to 800 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C/min in N2 atmosphere.

The antioxidant properties of different film samples were assessed using DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging methods. DPPH and ABTS solutions were prepared using the method described by Zheng et al. [31]. Briefly, 50 mg of the film was mixed with 5 mL of DPPH and ABTS solutions and maintained in darkness for 1 h at 37 °C. The absorbance of the mixture at 517 and 734 nm was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The radical-scavenging rate was calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)
Radical scavenging rate
%
=
A 0
−
A f
A 0
× 100
where A
0 and A
f are the absorbances of the DPPH and ABTS solutions without and with film, respectively.

The antibacterial activities of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) was measured using the plate counting method [32]. Briefly, film samples (4 cm × 4 cm) were sterilized using UV light for 20 min and subsequently immersed in 5 mL of bacterial suspension (106 CFU/mL). Then, the mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h at 120 rpm. A bacterial suspension without the film was used as a control. At preset intervals (3, 6, 9, and 12 h), 200 μL of the suspension was taken out, followed by successive dilution using sterile saline; subsequently, 100 μL of the diluent was inoculated and dispersed on Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plate. The total viable colonies of bacteria were counted after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. The antifungal activity of the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films against Penicillium palitans (P. palitans) and Aspergillus fumigatus (A. fumigatus) was evaluated using the Oxford-cup method. The steps involved are described in detail in our previous report [33]. The fungal growth inhibition rate was calculated after incubation at 28 °C for 72 h.

The fluorescence method was employed to investigate the potential of the PCDs-added composite films to generate ⋅OH radicals upon exposure to visible light irradiation [34]. TPA was dissolved in a 2 mM NaOH solution to form the reaction solution (0.5 mM), whereas 5 mg of the film samples were immersed into 30 mL of the TPA reaction solution and irradiated with a white LED lamp (visible light, power of 8 W) for 60 min at a distance of 15 cm. The fluorescence emission intensity of the produced 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA) at 430 nm under a 325 nm excitation wavelength was monitored using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at a time interval of 10 min.

The oxidative bleaching of the DPBF method was used to estimate the generation of ⋅O2 − radicals in the PCD-added composite films under visible light [35]. DPBF is quencher of the ⋅O2 − radicals. First, DPBF was dissolved in DMF to prepare a 10 mM DPBF solution. Subsequently, 30 mL of the film sample suspension (containing 150 mg film) was mixed with 100 μL of DPBF solution, and the reaction solution was exposed to visible light (LED) for 6 min. The fluorescence emission intensity of the reaction mixture at 485 nm under the excitation at 365 nm was measured after 60 s.

The overall migration of the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films into different food simulants (water, 3 % acetic acid, 50 % ethanol, and n-hexane) was examined via the method of Fortunati et al. [36]. In briefly, 2 cm × 3 cm film samples were placed in 10 mL of simulant (water, 3 % acetic acid, and 50 % ethanol) and maintained at 40 °C for 10 d, whereas the films in n-hexane were incubated at 38 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the films were taken out and the simulants were dried at 150 °C in an oven overnight. The residues were weighed and the overall migration value was expressed as milligram/kg of the simulant. Each film sample was tested in triplicate.

Strawberries were employed to estimate the packaging performance of the as-prepared composite films; in particular, strawberries with uniform size, homogeneous color, and no damage were selected. The strawberries were randomly classified into three tested groups and packaged with different materials (PE film, FSG/ADA, and FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 %). Untreated strawberries were used as control group. All the groups were maintained at room temperature (∼25 °C) for 7 d. The visible appearance, nutritional index (weight loss, titratable acid, total soluble solids, and vitamin C), and microbial count of each group were investigated throughout the storage period. Each measurement was performed in triplicate. The testing methods are described in detail in our previous study [30].

All data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD). The significant differences (P < 0.05) of each mean value were evaluated using the analysis of variance (ANOVA) following Duncan's multiple range test. For statistical analysis, SPSS 26.0 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA) was used.

TEM was performed to observe the morphological characteristics of the PCDs, and the TEM image (Fig. 2A) showed that the PCDs were approximately spherical particles that dispersed evenly without any aggregation. The high-resolution TEM image showed that the PCDs were crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.140 nm (inset of Fig. 2A), which is consistent with previous findings by Qi et al. [27]. The distribution range of the particle size was between 1.0 and 4.0 nm, with an average size of 2.37 ± 0.61 nm (Fig. 2B). As shown in Fig. 2C, the PCDs exhibited a yellow–brown color in visible light and a blue color when exposed to UV light at 365 nm owing to the quantum confinement effect, indicating that the PCDs were successfully synthesized by the carbonization of pomelo peel. Moreover, the UV–vis spectrum of the PCD solution showed a strong absorption peak at around 282 nm, which is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO or the C–OH bond [37], indicating a high UV-absorption ability, which is crucial for satisfying the requirement of UV-blocking packaging.

The fluorescence properties of PCDs were investigated via fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2C shows that the PCDs emitted the strongest fluorescence at 463 nm under excitation at 344 nm. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak of the PCDs shifted to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased from 314 to 374 nm (Fig. 2D), indicating the strong excitation wavelength-dependent response of the PCDs [38,39]. Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of the PCDs was determined to be 27.70 % as compared to 54.6 % QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4.

The XRD pattern of PCDs displayed two broad diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 8.04° and 22.34° (Fig. 2E), suggesting an amorphous structure [40]. FTIR spectroscopy was conducted to analyze the chemical functional groups of the PCDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2F. The band at 3254 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of OH, while the peak at approximately 2930 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CH. The peak at approximately 1667 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO. The peaks at 1583, 1278, 1046, and 678 cm−1 corresponded to chemical groups CC and C–OH as well as C–O–C and CH (alkyne) vibrations [24,41], respectively. These results confirmed the presence of carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the PCD surface, which may be derived from the carbohydrates in the pomelo peel.

The antimicrobial activity of PCDs was investigated using the inhibition zone method. As shown in Fig. 2G, the PCDs (100 mg/mL) exhibited an inhibition zone diameter of 3.53 mm for E. coli and 3.43 mm for L. monocytogenes, respectively. The antioxidant activities of PCDs were determined using DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays. As shown in Fig. 2H, the antioxidant activity of PCDs exhibited a concentration-dependent pattern, and the radical-scavenging rates increased with the PCD concentration. The IC50 values of DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity were measured to be 45.9 and 28.4 μg/mL, respectively, demonstrating its strong antioxidant activity, which is ascribed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.

SEM was used to observe the surface morphologies of the films, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 . The pure FSG film exhibited a smooth and homogeneous surface (Fig. 3A). As ADA was mixed with FSG, the FSG/ADA composite film displayed a slightly rough surface with many micro-voids (Fig. 3B, as marked by the red circles), which was similar to Maroufi's results [42], suggesting the generation of a crosslinked network structure between FSG and ADA. Owing to the addition of PCDs to the FSG/ADA matrix, the surfaces of composite films became denser and rougher (Fig. 3C-F). The FSG/ADA/PCDs-1 % film exhibited a homogenous surface, while FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film also showed a relatively homogenous surface although a few aggregations were observed. This indicates that PCDs with a low content can be dispersed uniformly and is compatible with the matrix. As the PCD content increased to 5 wt% and 7 wt%, numerous granular substances appeared on the FSG/ADA/PCDs film surface owing to the PCDs aggregations, indicating poor compatibility between the PCDs and biopolymers.

The crystalline behaviors of the composite films were investigated using XRD. As shown in Fig. 4A, a broad diffraction band at about 2θ of 21.5° was observed in all films, which corresponded to the amorphous structure of FSG and ADA [43]. By introducing the PCDs, a diffraction peak emerged at around 7.58°, which corresponded to the amorphous characteristic peak of PCDs (as shown in Fig. 2E) [40]. Moreover, the diffraction peak intensity at 21.5° of the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films did not change significantly when the PCDs content was <3 wt% compared with that of the FSG/ADA composite film, demonstrating that the adding PCDs at a low content did not alter the crystalline structure of FSG/ADA film owing to the good dispersibility of PCDs throughout the polymer matrix, as revealed by the SEM results. The peak intensity of FSG/ADA/PCDs films with high PCDs content weakened, indicating that the crystalline state of FSG/ADA film was damaged. Additionally, this corroborated the SEM findings that poor compatibility of high content PCDs and biopolymer matrix.

As shown in Fig. 4B, the alginate spectrum exhibited peaks at 3292 and 2924 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH, respectively. The peaks at 1604 and 1411 cm−1 were related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of -COO− groups, respectively. By contrast, the oxidation resulted in the appearance of two characteristic absorption peaks at around 1732 and 879 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of ADA, which were ascribed to aldehyde symmetric vibrations and hemiacetal bonds formed by aldehyde groups and adjacent hydroxyl groups, respectively [44]. Moreover, the chemical interactions among the film constituents were investigated via FTIR analysis (Fig. 4C). The pure FSG films presented obvious peaks at around 3295 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1, attributing to the stretching vibration of NH in amide A and the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH in amide B, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CO for amide I, the deformation vibrations of NH for amide II, and the CN plane and NH stretching vibrations for amide III were responsible for the absorption peaks at 1640, 1544, and 1238 cm−1, respectively. For the FSG/ADA film, the characteristic peaks of amide bands shifted to a lower wavenumber because of the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the FSG and ADA. Moreover, the peaks at 1627 and 1554 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN, suggesting the formation of Schiff base bonds between the FSG and ADA [45]. Moreover, the peak at 1026 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-O-C in the ADA macromolecules [46]. The FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films displayed spectra analogous to those of the FSG/ADA film, albeit with a lower peak intensity because of the interactions (such as van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonding) between the PCDs and biopolymer matrix [47]. These findings are in accordance with those reported by Ezati et al. [48], who incorporated N-functionalized CDs into cellulose nanofiber-based composite films for active packaging.

Food packaging materials must possess appropriate mechanical properties to ensure the effective protection of food during storage, shipping, and sales. The mechanical parameters of different films, including TS, EM, and EB, are showed in Fig. 5A-C. The pure FSG film exhibited a TS value of 14.02 ± 1.08 MPa and good ductility, as indicated by an EB value of 21.67 ± 3.43 %, which are consistent with those of FSG films synthesized by Campalani et al. [49]. Compared with the pure FSG film, the FSG/ADA binary composite film showed a significant improvement in mechanical strength benefitting from the formed Schiff base bonds between FSG and ADA. In contrast to the FSG/ADA composite film, the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films displayed a significant increase (P < 0.05) in the TS and EM. However, their EB decreased significantly, as similarly reported by Min et al. [50], owing to the formation of abundant hydrogen bonds between the fillers and biopolymer matrix [51]. Moreover, the TS and EM first increased and then decreased as the PCDs content increased, and the highest TS (33.63 ± 3.47 MPa) and EM (1020.34 ± 77.26 MPa) were related to the film with 1 % of PCDs. These results are mainly attributed to the fact that the 1 wt% PCDs can be dispersed evenly in the biopolymer matrix as shown in SEM image, thus resulting in the best compatibility and the formation of abundant hydrogen bonding interactions [52]. Furthermore, Fig. S1A of Supplementary Materials shows a comparison of the mechanical performances of the FSG-based composite films. The FSG-based prepared composite films in this study exhibited significantly better tensile properties while maintaining the appropriate stiffness and ductility, demonstrating their superiority than most other FSG-based composite films.

The water vapor barrier capability of food packaging is crucial for food preservation because it retards water vapor exchange between foodstuffs and their surroundings [53]. Fig. 5D displays the WVP of different composite films. Compared with the results of the FSG/ADA film, the WVP of FSG/ADA/PCDs films reduced significantly (P < 0.05) at low PCDs content (1 wt% and 3 wt%) but increased minimally (P > 0.05) at high PCDs content (5 wt% and 7 wt%). The initial decrease in the WVP is ascribed to the formation of a tortuous diffusion path for water vapor, decreased free volume between the biopolymer molecular chains, and enhanced the air-tightness of the films, which resulted from hydrogen bonding interactions between biopolymers and PCDs. The subsequent increase in the WVP is attributed to the existence of abundant free hydroxyl groups in the PCDs, which contribute to their hydrophilic nature. Khoshkalampour et al. [54] indicated a similar trend in the WVP when the hydrophilic CDs derived from grape leaves were added to the gelatin/oxidized Persian gum film. Fig. S1B of Supplementary Materials shows a comparison of the WVP between the reported FSG-based composite film and the as-prepared FSG-based composite films, which suggests a moderate water vapor barrier compared with those of most other FSG-based films. Although the WVP of our film is not the lowest owing to the hydrophilicity of the PCDs, its low cost, good mechanical properties, and biodegradability renders it a promising option.

Water resistance is a crucial factor in food packaging for preserving moisture and maintaining the integrity of the packaging material, as evaluated based on the WS and WCA. The results are presented in Fig. 5E-F. The WS and WCA of FSG/ADA were 42.95 % and 76.2°, respectively. By adding PCDs to FSG/ADA, the WS of PCDs-added films decreased, and the WCA of PCDs-added films increased. Among all the PCDs-added films, the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film indicated the lowest WS of 35.52 % and the highest WCA of 97.1°. This result is attributed to the relatively homogenous distribution of PCDs (3 wt%) in the composite films, which facilitated the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PCDs and biopolymers, thereby effectively reducing the free hydrophilic groups in the films [55]. A small number of aggregations occurred on the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film could provide proper surface roughness, which was also another possible reason for the increased WCA. The results SEM analysis corroborated this finding.

The color of food packaging films significantly affect the appearance of products and consumer preferences. The visual appearances of films are shown in Fig. 6A. The FSG/ADA film is highly transparent, whereas the PCDs-added composite films are slightly yellow. The yellow color of the composite films darkened gradually as the PCDs contents increased, whereas the green plant under the films remained clearly visible when ≤3 wt% of PCDs were added. The color of the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films was uniform throughout the films, further suggesting an even distribution of PCDs particles in the biopolymer matrix. The color parameters of the films are summarized in Table 1 . By incorporating the PCDs, the brightness (L value) of the FSG/ADA film reduced significantly, whereas both the redness (a value) and yellowness (b value) enhanced, which consequently increased the ΔE. Similar results have been reported in the literature [50].

Light radiation, especially UV light, significantly affects food quality via photolytic and photo-oxidative reactions. The light transmittance spectra of films were monitored in the 200–800 nm range, as shown in Fig. 6B. The transmittances of the films at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) were used to evaluate the UV-blocking capability and transparency of the films, as listed in Table 1. The FSG/ADA film exhibited high T280 and T660 values, indicating a low UV barrier and high transparency. By adding PCDs to FSG/ADA, the T280 values of the composite films reduced significantly, demonstrating their improved UV-blocking capability, which was attributable to the UV absorption capability of PCDs, as described above. The UV-blocking capacity of FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % increased significantly by 99.44 % compared with that of the FSG/ADA film. In addition, the T660 values (transparency) reduced significantly (P < 0.05) as the PCDs content increased, indicating that the introduction of PCDs significantly improved the UV-blocking capability, although at the expense of the film transparency. The UV-blocking property of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films are useful for protecting packaged foods against photooxidation and photodegradation induced by UV light.

Fluorescent/photoluminescent materials are the main materials used for the anti-counterfeit packaging, which can be used to identify counterfeit medicinal and food products. In this study, the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of FSG/ADA/PCDs film-forming solution were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6C. The FSG/ADA film-forming solution showed a clear excitation peak at 335 nm and an emission peak at 457 nm due to the fluorescence properties of the incorporated PCDs. Compared with the pure PCDs, the FSG/ADA/PCDs film-forming solution exhibited excitation and the emission peaks with slight blue shifts. The results above are attributed to the interaction between the biopolymers and PCDs, which influenced the fluorescence properties of PCDs, as similarly reported by Patil et al. [56]. The fluorescence emission spectra of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films with different PCDs content are recorded in Fig. 6D. The FSG/ADA composite film without PCDs did not indicate any characteristic fluorescence emission peak when excited at 335 nm. By contrast, the FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films exhibited a clear fluorescence emission peak at around 460 nm, and the intensity of fluorescence emission peak increased with the PCDs content. As show in Fig. 6E, the corresponding optical photographs of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films presented an intense blue-light-emitting appearance under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. These results indicated that the PCD-added films are promising candidates for anti-counterfeiting packaging application.

The TGA and DTG thermal curves are plotted in Fig. 7 . Based on the TGA curve, the FSG/ADA composite film showed a three-step thermodynamic decomposition process. The initial thermo-decomposition was observed at 50 °C–120 °C, which was due to moisture evaporation from the film [57]. The second thermo-decomposition happened at 130 °C–250 °C, with the fastest decomposition recorded at 225 °C, which was assigned to the decomposition of glycerol, ADA, and lower molecular weight protein in the FSG. The third step began at about 250 °C and terminated at about 400 °C, with the fastest decomposition temperature recorded at 348 °C, which was due to the decomposition of higher molecular weight protein in the FSG. When PCDs were incorporated into the FSG/ADA film, the thermal decomposition patterns of the films changed significantly. During the initial stage, the PCD-added composite films degraded more slowly due to more moisture entrapped in the hydrophilic PCDs. During the second stage, the thermal decomposition rate of PCD-added films decreased when the PCDs content was <3 wt% and then increased as the PCDs content further increased to 7 wt%. This is associated with the PCDs content of <3 wt% facilitates the formation of abundant hydrogen bonding between the biopolymer and PCDs, thus effectively improving the thermal stability of films [58]. During the third stage, the thermal decomposition of PCD-added films accelerated as the PCDs content increased owing to the decomposition of the carbon-based structure of PCDs.

Excellent food packaging materials are required to inhibit the oxidation of protein and lipid present in food. Compared with adding antioxidants directly to food, incorporating antioxidants into packaging materials is more appealing because it avoids issues associated with organoleptic alterations. The antioxidant activity of films were assessed using DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . The FSG/ADA film showed the lowest antioxidant activity, with a 10.08 % radical scavenging rate for DPPH and 14.94 % for ABTS, owing to the presence of free hydroxyl and amino groups as well as the formed Schiff base [17]. The antioxidant activity of FSG/ADA/PCDs films improved significantly (P < 0.05) as the PCDs content increased. The FSG/ADA/PCDs films (3 wt%, 5 wt% and 7 wt%) displayed nearly 100 % ABTS radical scavenging ability and 52.59 %, 73.82 %, and 91.71 % DPPH radical scavenging abilities, respectively. These improvements can be ascribed to the existence of multiple reductive electron-donating groups (−OH, O=C − OH) on the PCDs surface [59], which can directly react with free radicals and act as antioxidants. Moreover, the higher antioxidant ability assessed via the ABTS method compared with that of the DPPH method is ascribed to the superior interaction of the aqueous ABTS solution with the hydrophilic PCDs [60].

The antimicrobial capability is a critical characteristic of food packaging because it inhibits pathogenic contamination and preserves food quality. The antimicrobial activity of different film samples was estimated by the total colony counting and Oxford-cup methods. The antimicrobial activities of films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes are exhibited in Fig. 9A-B. The FSG/ADA film displayed a slight bacteriostatic effect on both tested bacteria, i.e., it reduced E. coli by 5.4 Log CFU/mL and L. monocytogenes by 6.1 Log CFU/mL relative to the control group after 12h of incubation. This is ascribed to a certain antimicrobial activity of the Schiff base formed between FSG and ADA, which is in line with previously published reports [61]. On the contrary, the FAG/ADA/PCDs film exhibited obvious antimicrobial property towards both test bacteria, which enhanced as the PCDs content increased. By adding PCDs to FSG/ADA, the growth and proliferation of bacteria suppressed significantly as well as exhibited a bactericidal effect. At a low PCDs content (1 wt%), the composite film suppressed the bacterial growth after 3 and 6 h of incubation, whereas it indicated bactericidal ability against E. coli after 9 h of incubation and against L. monocytogenes after 12 h of incubation, respectively. The FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film completely inhibited the growth of E. coli after 6 h and growth of L. monocytogenes after 9 h, respectively. Specifically, the FSG/ADA/PCDs-5 % film and FSG/ADA/PCDs-7 % film killed most of the two tested bacteria after only 6 h of incubation, demonstrating their potent inhibitory effect on the bacterial growth. Furthermore, the PCDs-added films exhibited superior antimicrobial activity against E. coli than against L. monocytogenes, which is attributed to the differences in the bacterial cell wall structure. The presence of thick and rigid peptidoglycan layers in the Gram-positive bacteria provides high resistance to external attack [62,63]. Similar result was reported by Walia et al. [64], who synthesized CDs that emitted bright blue and red light as a fluorescent imaging probe.

In addition, Fig. 9C-D exhibited the growth inhibition rates of different composite films against P. palitans and A. fumigatus. The FSG/ADA film showed a slight antifungal activity, with the growth inhibition rates against P. palitans of 7.21 % and against A. fumigatus of 8.42 %. The PCD-added films exhibited an obvious antifungal activity against both fungi, and the fungal growth inhibition rate was proportional to the PCDs conten. When the content of PCDs increased to 7 wt%, the fungal growth inhibition rate of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite film against both fungi reached 92.9 %. Overall, these results demonstrated the excellent antimicrobial activities of FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films against both bacteria and fungi, showing their potential application in active food packaging.

To date, ROS production via photo-excitation is a major underlying mechanism associated with the antimicrobial activity of CDs. ROS, such as hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and superoxide anion radicals (⋅O2 −), can bind to the bacterial surfaces either directly or through electrostatic interactions. They interact with proteins, lipids, and nucleotides, caused the denaturation of membrane proteins; consequently, the permeability of cell membrane is increased and severe cellular physiological damage is resulted, thus ultimately leading to cell lysis and death [65]. To evaluate the ROS generation of PCDs-added films under visible light irradiation, the changes in fluorescence signal for HTPA formation [34] and DPBF quenching [35] were separately studied to determine the generation of ⋅OH and ⋅O2 −, and the results are displayed in Fig. 9. Notably, non-fluorescent TPA responds to photogenic ⋅OH to generate fluorescent HTPA. As shown in Fig. 9E, as the PCD content increased, the fluorescence intensity of PCD-added films increased, indicating the generation of ⋅OH radicals. The colorimetric fluorescent probe DPBF, which is a singlet oxygen quencher, was subjected to oxidative bleaching in the presence of ⋅O2 − radicals to produce colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene. The reduction in intensity of characteristic fluorescence emission band at 485 nm offers evidence of ⋅O2 − generation by the films. As presented in Fig. 9F, the FSG/ADA film exhibited the highest and stable emission intensity, demonstrating the absence of ⋅O2 − generation. In contrast, the PCDs-added films showed a significant reduction in DPBF emission intensity at 485 nm within 6 min, and the decrease rate of emission intensity of DPBF accelerated as the content of PCDs increased, demonstrating the generation of ⋅O2 − radicals and an increase in ⋅O2 − production with an increased amount of PCDs. This finding is agreed well with the study by Riahi et al. [47], who found that the antimicrobial activity of CMC-based films enhanced with the addition of chitosan-based CDs.

The inertness of food packaging materials is important when exposed to food. The migration characteristics of food packaging films into food simulants were investigated. The overall migration values of films into different food simulants (water simulating aqueous food, 3 % acetic acid simulating acid food, 50 % ethanol simulating alcoholic food, and n-hexane simulating lipid food) were measured. The results are summarized in Table 2 . All the film samples displayed an overall migration in n-hexane below the allowable limit of 60 mg/kg simulant [66]. While the values in other three food simulants exceeded the threshold level due to the hydrophilicity of the matrix components and PCDs. These results are attributable to the fact that highly hydrophilic PCDs increased the affinity between aqueous food simulants and composite films. Additionally, among the three aqueous food simulants, the overall migration values of films in water were higher than those in other two simulants; this might be due to polar groups on the PCDs surfaces facilitated the interaction of the hydrophilic simulant with film samples [67]. As the PCDs introducing into the FSG/ADA, the values in all food simulants exhibited a trend of first decrease and then increased, and the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % had the lowest overall migration values. It could be associated with the formation of plentiful hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of PCDs and the biopolymer matrix at low PCDs content, while the more content of PCDs contributed to the high solubility of the composite film, as mentioned above.

Fresh strawberries are highly popular among consumers due to their unique flavor and rich bioactive substances. However, the strawberries are highly perishable after postharvest, it has been reported that the fresh strawberries will deteriorate and decay in about 1–3 d under ambient conditions if without appropriate preservation treatment. In this study, we selected the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % composite film, which exhibited the best functional performance, to preserve fresh strawberries preservation under room temperature. The changes in appearance and postharvest quality of strawberries were depicted in Fig. 10 during storage.

As illustrated in Fig. 10A, the strawberries in control group began to rot on day 3 due to microbial infection and completely decayed on day 7. In contrast, the deterioration strawberries in PE and FSG/ADA groups occurred on day 5 and day 7 of storage, respectively. This could be attributed to the barrier properties of PE and antimicrobial activity in some degree of FSG/ADA film. The strawberries in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group did not indicate significantly spoilage or microbial damage on day 7; hence, the edibility and commercial value of the strawberries were preserved.

Strawberries are extremely prone to rapid weight loss due to their thin skin and high respiration and transpiration rates, which can induce shrinkage and decrease their freshness. The weight loss rate of strawberries in different groups are illustrated in Fig. 10B. The strawberries in control group showed the highest weight loss rate due to the easily escape of moisture to the surroundings, as no protection was implemented, whereas the strawberries in PE group indicated the lowest weight loss rate attributing to superior water vapor resistance of PE film. The weight loss rates of strawberries in FSG/ADA and FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % groups were lower than those in control groups, attributing to water vapor resistance properties of these composite films. Compared with FSG/ADA group, the weight loss rate of strawberries in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group reduced by 30.60 % on day 7, owing to decreased water vapor permeability of film through addition of PCDs as mentioned above.

The titratable acid (TA) content directly affects the flavor of strawberries. The changes in TA of strawberries with storage time are depicted in Fig. 10C. The TA content of strawberries in control group exhibited a continuing downward trend for the 7 days storage, mainly owing to microbial infection. Conversely, the TA content of strawberries in packaged groups first increased on day 1 and then decreased, which was attributed to maturity of the picked strawberries on the first day and subsequent respiration and metabolism. On the day 7, the TA content in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group was 0.56 g/100 g, while that in control group delinked to 0.43 g/100 g, indicating that the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film could effectively retard the decrease rate of TA content in strawberries. This finding was associated with the better antimicrobial activity and gas barrier of the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film, which reduced microbial infection and improved the packaging internal environment, thereby reducing the respiration intensity of strawberries and decreased the consumption of TA as a respiration substrate.

Total soluble solid (TSS) are essential indicator for determining the nutritional quality of strawberries. As illustrated in Fig. 10D, the TSS content of all strawberries displayed an increase in the first day due to the further increase of ripeness. However, the TSS content in control group presented a continuous rise trend before day 5, and then decreased sharply on day 7, mainly attributing to severe moisture loss in the control group caused increased TSS content [68]. For all the packaged groups, the TSS content exhibited a continuous declining trend after the first day, which is because the strawberries expended their own nutrients to sustain respiration. Compared with fresh strawberries, the TSS content in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group reduced by 15.02 % on the day 7, i.e., 36.63 % higher than that in the control group on the same day. These findings indicated the respiration of strawberries in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group was effectively inhibited, thus reducing the consumption of soluble solid.

Strawberries contain a large amount of vitamin C, which is a considerable advantage for their popularity among consumers. The variation in the vitamin C content of strawberries is depicted in Fig. 10E. At the first days, the vitamin C content of all strawberries gradually increased as they matured. Afterward, a significant reduction in vitamin C content were observed in all strawberries throughout the storage time, owing to the self-respiration, extrinsic oxidation, and microorganism invasion. Compared with the control group, the packaged groups showed higher vitamin C content due to the barrier property of the packaging material. Moreover, the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group exhibited the highest vitamin C content of 68.62 mg/100 g at the end of storage, demonstrating their ability in effectively delaying the depletion of vitamin C.

The delicate skin of strawberries renders them susceptible to microbial attacks and thus rotting. As shown in Fig. 10F, all strawberries showed a progressive increase in the total aerobic bacterial counts throughout the storage period, with an initial count of 1.32 log CFU/g. In the control group, the total aerobic bacterial counts were higher than those in packaged groups for the first 3 days and then reached 7.80 log CFU/g at the end of the storage, which exceeded the threshold limitation of 7.0 log CFU/g [69]. In contrast, the bacterial counts in the PE group dramatically rose on day 5. This finding could be explained that the water vapor generated by the respiration of strawberries condensed on the surface of strawberries and the internal surface of PE film, which produced a high-humidity internal environment and thus contributed to rapid bacterial growth. The total aerobic bacterial count in the FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % group exhibited the slowest increasing trend among all the packaged groups due to the excellent antimicrobial capability of this composite film. By the end of the storage, the total aerobic bacterial count in FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % was 4.74 log CFU/g, a reduction of 3.06 log CFU/g compared to that of the control group. In conclusion, it concludes that the resulting FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film could delay the deterioration of strawberry quality and prolong the postharvest life for 7 days at room temperature.

In this study, ADA was utilized to reinforce the mechanical strength of FSG films through a Shiff-based crosslinking reaction between aldehyde and amine groups. PCDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and served as active fillers for the FSG/ADA film. The PCDs were spherical and measured 2.37 ± 0.61 nm, and they were well compatible with the biopolymer matrix, as revealed by SEM, FTIR, and XRD analyses. The composite films containing different contents of PCDs were systematically characterized based on their morphological, mechanical, optical, thermal, and functional properties. The FSG/ADA/PCDs composite films exhibited enhanced favorable mechanical properties, excellent UV-blocking and water-vapor barrier properties, and high thermal performance. Additionally, the FSG/ADA/PCDs films exhibited favorable antioxidant activity against DPPH and ABTS radicals, effectively antimicrobial effects against bacteria and fungi. The FSG/ADA/PCDs-3 % film, which exhibited comprehensive physicochemical and functional performance, was employed for strawberry preservation at room temperature. Results showed that the composite film effectively prolonged the postharvest life of strawberries to 7 days by reducing their weight and nutritional loss and inhibiting microorganism growth. Hence, we believed that the synthesized FSG/ADA/PCDs films are a promising candidate for active food packaging.

Yongshi Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Junxian Yang: Investigation, Validation. Linping Sun: Software, Supervision, Data curation. Bingzhen Liu: Investigation.
